ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKM covers chicken burger/sandwich composite; used as closest defensible match for spicy tomato chicken burger.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No sufficiently specific SNOMED CT concept identified for spicy tomato chicken burger; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is approximately 180 g, representing a single assembled spicy tomato chicken burger as served in a Singapore fast-food or casual-dining context.

nutrient_source.title: Spicy Tomato Chicken Burger — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Spicy Tomato Chicken Burger (cr_id G00010113). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is not reported in the HPB entry and is estimated from USDA FoodData Central reference for a comparable fried chicken burger (USDA SR Legacy item 'Fast foods, chicken patty sandwich'); see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Spicy Tomato Chicken Burger. GI estimated at 70 (high) based on the dominant carbohydrate vehicle being a refined white-flour burger bun, analogous to white bread (GI ~70–75, University of Sydney GI Database) and consistent with fast-food chicken sandwich composites reported in Atkinson et al. 2021. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (70) × available carbohydrate per serving (29.57 g/100g × 180 g serving = 53.2 g) ÷ 100 = 37.3 g GL.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium at 487 mg/100g with low potassium offset (197 mg/100g); sodium-to-potassium ratio is unfavourable for blood pressure management.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this burger; if consumed, request reduced spicy tomato sauce and no mayonnaise to lower sodium intake, and pair with a potassium-rich side such as a banana or leafy greens.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI refined-flour bun (GI ~70) combined with 29.6 g/100g carbohydrate and only 1.8 g/100g dietary fibre produces rapid postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this burger due to its high glycaemic load (GL ~37 per serving); if consumed, choose a smaller portion, request a grilled chicken option, and pair with non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Combination of high sodium (487 mg/100g), estimated saturated fat (~2.5 g/100g), trans fat (~0.1 g/100g from deep-frying), and cholesterol (29 mg/100g) presents multiple cardiovascular risk factors.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should minimise consumption of this burger; the deep-fried preparation, trans fat content, and high sodium load collectively elevate LDL cholesterol and blood pressure risk, and a grilled chicken alternative with reduced sauce is strongly preferred.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; trans fat (~0.1 g/100g) is a hard pro-inflammatory disqualifier.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this burger in its standard deep-fried form; AGE generation from deep-frying and the presence of trans fat are strong pro-inflammatory factors, and a grilled chicken preparation without tomato sauce (nightshade) is the only acceptable alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Spicy Tomato Chicken Burger is a Western-style fast-food burger adapted for the Singapore market, consisting of a deep-fried battered chicken patty served in a refined white-flour bun with a smooth, tangy spicy tomato sauce, lettuce, and mayonnaise. The chicken patty is coated in a seasoned batter and deep-fried to a crispy golden exterior, yielding a juicy interior with a warm, mildly spicy flavour profile from the tomato-based sauce. Per 100 g, the burger provides 263 kcal, 13.3 g protein, 10.2 g fat, and 29.6 g carbohydrate, with 487 mg sodium — placing it in the high-sodium category. The refined bun contributes a high glycaemic index (~70), making the overall glycaemic load substantial (~37 per standard 180 g serving). Clinically, this burger is unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension (high sodium, low potassium offset), Type 2 Diabetes (high GI, low fibre, high GL), or cardiovascular disease (trans fat, saturated fat, high sodium). The deep-fried preparation generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), making it additionally unsuitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients. It is commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner item in Singapore fast-food and casual-dining settings and is classified as a processed bread-based dish in the grains and staples food group.
common_user_queries: Is spicy tomato chicken burger healthy?
Calories in spicy tomato chicken burger
Sodium content of chicken burger Singapore
Is chicken burger suitable for hypertension?
Is spicy tomato chicken burger halal?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients and micronutrients sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010113, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy fast-food chicken sandwich reference. GI estimated by analogy to refined white-flour bread from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021. All values are internally consistent and nutritionally plausible.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (2.5 g/100g) is not reported in the HPB source; estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 'Fast foods, chicken patty sandwich' (NDB 21120) as the closest approved-source analogue. 2. Trans fat (0.1 g/100g) is inferred from deep-frying process and is consistent with the input health_suitability notes; not explicitly reported in HPB source. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, full vitamin panel, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this specific Singapore fast-food burger are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 4. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on the refined white-flour bun as the dominant carbohydrate vehicle; no direct GI measurement for this specific product was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. Halal status is assumed based on typical Singapore fast-food chicken burger preparation; verification against the specific vendor's halal certification is recommended. 6. Serving size of 180 g is an estimate for a standard Singapore fast-food chicken burger; actual weight may vary by vendor.
